text:
Admission Date:  1987-03-27       Discharge Date:  2003-05-27

Date of Birth:   1976-11-05       Sex:  F

Service:

HIScottOF PRESENT ILLNESS:  Briefly, this is a 61-year-old
female transferred from an outside hospital for elective
cardiac catheterization.  She has a known history of aortic
stenosis for nine years.  Catheterization in 2013 which
showed an aortic valve diameter of 1.3 cm, but no history of
coronary artery disease.  She had some increased fatigue and
had a decrease in her valve area.  She was scheduled for an
aortic valve replacement with Dr. [**Last Name (Prefixes) **]; however, she
presented for preoperative cardiac catheterization to assess
her coronary anatomy.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:  (Past medical history is significant
for)
1.  Osteoarthritis.
2.  Hypertension.
3.  High cholesterol.
4.  Aortic stenosis.
5.  Status post parathyroidectomy in 2008.

ALLERGIES:  She has an allergy to PENICILLIN and INTRAVENOUS
DYE.

MEDICATIONS ON ADMISSION:  Her medications included
atenolol 25 mg p.o. q.d., desipramine 10 mg p.o. q.h.s.,
multivitamin p.o. q.d., Ativan p.o. as needed

FAMILY HISTORY:  Family history was negative for premature
coronary artery disease.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION ON PRESENTATION:  On physical
examination, she was afebrile.  Her vital signs were stable.
She had a normal sinus rhythm.  Her neck was supple with no
jugular venous distention.  Her lungs were clear to
auscultation bilaterally.  Heart had a regular rate and
rhythm with a normal first heart sound.  She had a 3/6
systolic ejection murmur that radiated to her carotids.  Her
abdomen was nontender and nondistended.  Bowel sounds were
present.  Her extremities were warm and well perfused with 1+
pulses bilaterally with no bruits.

HOSPITAL COURSE:   She was admitted on 1987-03-27 and
taken to the operating room on 2014-02-13 where an
aortic valve replacement was performed.

The patient was transferred to the Cardiothoracic Surgery
Recovery Unit postoperatively.  She was unable to be awoken
or aroused postoperatively, and was unable to be weaned from
the ventilator.  Her chest tube was removed due to a low
output.  However, again, the patient was noted to not be able
to move the right side of her body.  She was responsive on
the left and was able to open her eyes.

At this time, a CT scan was performed which showed a possible
diffuse infarction of her cerebellum.  She was scheduled for
a magnetic resonance imaging which revealed a diffuse injury
to her brain.  She slowly began to regain function and was
able to be weaned from her ventilator at that time and was
successfully extubated.  She began to slowly move all four of
her extremities.  She was transfused one unit of blood for a
low hematocrit.

Neurology was consulted at that time for her stroke, and she
had a slight difference in the strength and motor reflexes on
her right side.  The patient was started on Plavix in
addition to her aspirin; per Neurology consultation.

Physical Therapy was consulted after extubation, when she
began to regain function, to assess her mobility and to
define her weaknesses and begin cardiac rehabilitation as
well as enhance her strength on her right side.  They
continued to see her and suggested cardiac rehabilitation
placement after medical care.

The patient continued to improve in the Intensive Care Unit,
and her Foley catheter was removed at that time.  The Plavix
dose was changed due to Neurology consultation.

The patient was transferred to the floor on 1980-01-25 in stable condition.  The patient continued to ambulate
and work with Physical Therapy, and Neurology continued to
follow along.

At the time of this dictation, the patient has been in stable
condition and was up moving around and working with Physical
Therapy, and the plan is to be discharged to rehabilitation.
Please check Addendum for date.

MEDICATIONS ON DISCHARGE:  (Her medications upon discharge
were)
1.  Plavix 75 mg p.o. b.i.d.
2.  Protonix 40 mg p.o. b.i.d.
3.  Motrin 400 mg p.o. q.4-6h. as needed.
4.  Enteric-coated aspirin 325 mg p.o. q.d.
5.  Amy Castillo 20 mEq p.o. b.i.d.
6.  Colace 100 mg p.o. b.i.d.
7.  Lasix 20 mg p.o. b.i.d.
8.  Lopressor 25 mg p.o. b.i.d.
9.  Vicodin one to two tablets p.o. q.4h. as needed.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSES:  (Her discharge diagnoses were as
follows)
1.  Coronary artery disease.
2.  Aortic stenosis; status post aortic valve replacement.
3.  Osteoarthritis.
4.  Hypertension.
5.  High cholesterol.
6.  Status post parathyroidectomy.

DISCHARGE DISPOSITION:  The patient was to be discharged to
rehabilitation.

CONDITION AT DISCHARGE:  Condition on discharge was stable.

DIRichE INSTRUCTIONS:
1.  The patient was instructed to follow up with her primary
care physician in one to two weeks.
2.  To follow up with Dr. [**Last Name (Prefixes) **] in four weeks.
3.  To follow up with Neurology as needed.

NOTE:  Please see Addendum for any changes to medications as
well as discharge date.




                            Davis [**Last Name (Prefixes) 413**], M.D.  42824

Dictated By:Higgins
MEDQUIST36
